# Decision Memo — Consolidating three internal tools into one platform

**To:** Engineering & Operations leadership   **From:** Platform team   **Date:** 2026-06-22
**Status:** Decision requested by 2026-07-06

*Tool names (TaskBoard, OpsLog, Reqs) are illustrative placeholders — swap in the real ones. Quantities tagged (est.) are planning assumptions, not measured figures; veto or correct them. Items in **Open questions** must be confirmed before Phase 1.*

## Recommendation
Consolidate **TaskBoard** (project tracking), **OpsLog** (incident tracking), and **Reqs** (request intake) onto one work-management platform over roughly two quarters (est.). Migrate in the order **Reqs → OpsLog → TaskBoard**, run each old tool read-only for ~30 days (est.) after cutover, then decommission. **Build on the existing TaskBoard codebase** rather than buying a new SaaS or starting from scratch.

**Decision requested:** approve the direction, the build-on-TaskBoard choice, and the staffing in *Cost*.

## Why now
The three tools overlap heavily on their data model (items, owners, status, comments) but don't share identity, search, or reporting. Concrete costs today: (1) the same incident is logged in OpsLog and re-keyed into TaskBoard by hand; (2) no cross-tool view, so weekly reporting is assembled manually; (3) three separate auth models and three vendor renewals. Two of the three are also nearing end of vendor support. The exact magnitudes are listed in *Open questions* and must be confirmed before commit — they are not assumed here.

## Options considered
- **A. Do nothing.** No cost now, but the manual reporting tax and re-keying continue, and the unsupported tools become a security/maintenance risk. *Rejected.*
- **B. Buy one new SaaS platform.** Fastest feature breadth, but forces a full migration off all three at once, simultaneous retraining, ongoing per-seat cost, and loss of our custom OpsLog automations. Higher risk, higher recurring cost.
- **C. Build on the existing TaskBoard codebase (recommended).** TaskBoard already owns the richest data model and our SSO. Extend it with intake forms (replacing Reqs) and an incident type with on-call hooks (replacing OpsLog). Incremental, one tool at a time, reuses auth and a familiar UI. Cost: engineering time, and TaskBoard's reporting layer needs work.

**Decision driver:** B's all-at-once migration is the main failure mode in tool consolidations; C lets us migrate and validate one tool at a time and roll back per-tool if a cutover goes wrong.

## Plan *(durations are estimates, firmed up at kickoff)*
1. **Phase 1 — Q3, ~6 wks (est.):** Add configurable intake forms; migrate Reqs; run Reqs read-only ~30 days. *Owner: Platform eng.*
2. **Phase 2 — late Q3–early Q4, ~6 wks (est.):** Add incident type + on-call/paging hooks; migrate OpsLog history; read-only ~30 days. *Owner: Platform eng + Ops lead.*
3. **Phase 3 — Q4, ~4 wks (est.):** Unify cross-tool reporting and saved views; decommission the two old tools; cancel renewals. *Owner: Platform eng + Data.*

## Success metrics
- Zero manual re-keying between incident and project tracking.
- Weekly reporting reduced to a single-source pull (set the target once the baseline in *Open questions* is measured).
- Two vendor contracts cancelled; one identity/permission model.
- No P1 regression in incident response time during/after Phase 2 (vs. trailing-quarter baseline).

## Risks & mitigations
- **Data loss on migration** → migrate read-only, keep the source live ~30 days, reconcile row counts before cutover.
- **OpsLog automations don't port cleanly** → spike the on-call hooks in week 1 of Phase 2 before committing.
- **Adoption resistance** → migrate the lowest-traffic tool (Reqs) first to prove the pattern; train per phase.
- **Scope creep on reporting** → fixed Phase 3 timebox; defer non-blocking features to a backlog.

## Cost *(estimate — confirm at kickoff)*
Roughly one engineer at half-time for ~two quarters (est.), plus ~2 weeks of Ops lead time in Phase 2 (est.). Intended to be offset by cancelled renewals and recovered reporting hours from the quarter after Phase 3; the actual offset depends on the figures in *Open questions*.

## Open questions *(need owner input before Phase 1)*
- Combined annual renewal cost and exact end-of-support dates for the two at-risk tools.
- Measured baseline: current weekly manual-reporting hours and data-model overlap, to set real targets.
- Whether any external/customer-facing integration depends on OpsLog's current API.

---

**One thing for you to confirm (the gate left it unchecked, by design):** altitude. I wrote this as a one-page exec decision memo — recommendation-first, light on implementation. If the real audience is the engineers who'll build it, it should drop a level into a build spec (data-model mapping, API/automation port details, migration scripts). And since you didn't name the actual three tools, I used placeholders and a build-on-the-strongest-existing-tool recommendation; if the real situation has no clear "richest" tool or a hard buy-vs-build constraint, tell me and I'll re-weigh options B and C.
